question.text: Scenario: Mr. Arvind Sharma, aged 42, is a senior manager at an IT company earning Rs. 18 lakh per annum (gross). His wife Mrs. Priya works part-time earning Rs. 6 lakh per annum. Their combined monthly expenses are Rs. 1,20,000. They have two children aged 14 and 10. Arvind's current investments include: Equity Mutual Funds Rs. 35 lakh, PPF Rs. 12 lakh (current balance), Bank Fixed Deposits Rs. 8 lakh, Employee PF balance Rs. 18 lakh, and a savings bank balance of Rs. 3 lakh. He has an outstanding home loan of Rs. 22 lakh with EMI of Rs. 25,000/month (included in expenses above) and no other loans. His employer contributes Rs. 1,800/month to PF and he contributes the same. He wants to retire at age 58 and needs a retirement corpus of Rs. 3 crore. He also needs Rs. 30 lakh for his elder child's education in 4 years and Rs. 25 lakh for the younger child in 8 years. Assume equity returns at 12% CAGR and debt returns at 7% CAGR. Inflation is 6% p.a. Question 2 of 5: What is the Sharma family's net worth?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Net Worth = Total Assets - Total Liabilities = Rs. 76 lakh - Rs. 22 lakh (home loan) = Rs. 54 lakh. The home itself is excluded in a conservative estimate since it's self-occupied. **The Trap:** Rs. 76 lakh is total investments, not net worth. Must deduct the outstanding home loan. **Cross-Exam Context:** Net worth calculation is a core X-A concept tested in Ch 3. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Net worth is the true measure of financial health — track it annually and show clients how it grows as loans are repaid and investments appreciate.
